EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of deep convection began to develop late in the afternoon of June 17th across the Ark-La-Tex region. A stationary front was draped from west-northwest to east-southeast through the region, with temperatures generally in the mid to upper 90s and dew points around 80 degrees. These conditions contributed to extreme instability with around 5500-6000 J/kg. As a result, vigorous updraft development occurred over the next few hours, eventually maturing into some supercell structures given 45-55 knots of bulk shear. The threat for damaging wind gusts and very large hail quickly materialized with a report of large hail near the Texas/Louisiana border before severe storms became much more numerous farther east into Northwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail reported in Oil City via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a naphtha storage tank at Calcasieu Refining.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tank at Calcasieu Refining Company igniting naphtha. The fire prompted evacuations for an area of 1.5 miles from the site including Graywood. A shelter in place was ordered for residents within 5 miles. The fire burned into the early morning of the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasingly warm and moist low-level southerly flow became enhanced along a retreating warm front as it advanced back north of the region on June 24th. Meanwhile, an upper level ridge of high pressure also expanded northeast from Mexico and into the Southern Plains and eventually right across the Ark-La-Tex region through the end of June. As a result, afternoon temperatures climbed into the mid and upper 90s to near 100 degrees over these areas. When combined with the high humidity, heat indices ranged from 110-119 degrees from the June 24th through the 30th. Unfortunately, a 49 year-old male collapsed on a sidewalk just after 5 pm June 25th in Shreveport (Caddo Parish). He was rushed to a local hospital where he died shortly afterwards. The Caddo Parish Coroner's Office determined that the cause of death was heat related.

A lingering upper-level trough stretching from the Texas Panhandle southeastward into Louisiana and along the northern Gulf coast provided the necessary forcing for isolated severe thunderstorms to develop across parts of North Louisiana late on June 4th. By the mid to late afternoon hours, thunderstorms began to develop in a modestly unstable air mass with the greatest coverage extending from Deep East Texas into portions of North Louisiana. A few thunderstorms reached severe limits with multiple local storm reports of large hail and damaging wind gusts scattered across North Louisiana throughout the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported in the Spearsville community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and moist low-level southerly winds became enhanced along and ahead of a stalled frontal boundary draped near to just north of the Interstate 20 corridor of East Texas and North Louisiana. An upper level ridge of high pressure also expanded northeast from Mexico across much of Texas. As a result, afternoon temperatures climbed into the mid to upper 90s over these areas. When combined with the high humidity, heat indices ranged from 110-119 degrees from the June 17th through the 20th. Although slightly cooler and less humid conditions moved into North Louisiana on June 21st, a 62 year old female was found dead in her home in Keithville (Southern Caddo Parish) following several days of no electricity after severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread power outages on June 16th. The Caddo Coroner's Office stated that the victim had suffered from previous health issues, and that the intense heat and humidity led to hyperthermia, and ultimately, her death.

Warm and moist west to southwest winds around 30 knots in the low levels helped to maintain a feed of unstable air from northern Texas into the Ark-La-Tex region through the early morning hours on June 18th. Strong surface-based instability just downstream of a developing thunderstorm complex resulted in an increase in severe potential through  the morning across parts of East Texas and North Louisiana. Additional thunderstorms did eventually become better organized, including further back-building toward the warmer surface instability, leading to numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging wind gusts across North Louisiana throughout the morning along with a few additional severe reports later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong size hail reported in Mooringsport via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms began to develop during the afternoon of June 3rd across parts of East and Northeast Texas, extending eastward into portions of Northwest Louisiana. Strong daytime heating and dew points in the middle 60s to lower 70s allowed for modest air mass destabilization, with as much as 1500-2000 J/kg of instability from East Texas into much of neighboring Northwest Louisiana. Deep layer flow was weak, resulting in slow moving storms and very little shear in the background environment. Given this regime, storms generally posed a brief severe risk and remained very pulse in nature, with an increasing threat of flash flooding throughout the afternoon. Numerous local storm reports were documented of large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. Unfortunately, one flash flood related fatality was reported when a male driver tried to drive through floodwaters on Hearne Avenue in Shreveport. The truck he was driving in quickly took on water, and while his passenger was able to escape, the driver drowned in the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail fell for approximately 15 to 20 minutes at the NWS Shreveport office with some brief accumulation on grass surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasingly warm and moist low-level southerly flow became enhanced along a retreating warm front as it advanced back north of the region on June 24th. Meanwhile, an upper level ridge of high pressure also expanded northeast from Mexico and into the Southern Plains and eventually right across the Ark-La-Tex region through the end of June. As a result, afternoon temperatures climbed into the mid and upper 90s to near 100 degrees over these areas. When combined with the high humidity, heat indices ranged from 110-119 degrees from the June 24th through the 30th. Unfortunately, a 49 year-old male collapsed on a sidewalk just after 5 pm June 25th in Shreveport (Caddo Parish). He was rushed to a local hospital where he died shortly afterwards. The Caddo Parish Coroner's Office determined that the cause of death was heat related.

Warm and moist west to southwest winds around 30 knots in the low levels helped to maintain a feed of unstable air from northern Texas into the Ark-La-Tex region through the early morning hours on June 18th. Strong surface-based instability just downstream of a developing thunderstorm complex resulted in an increase in severe potential through  the morning across parts of East Texas and North Louisiana. Additional thunderstorms did eventually become better organized, including further back-building toward the warmer surface instability, leading to numerous severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging wind gusts across North Louisiana throughout the morning along with a few additional severe reports later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported in the Tiburon subdivision via social media.

Very hot and moist low-level southerly winds became enhanced along and ahead of a stalled frontal boundary draped near to just north of the Interstate 20 corridor of East Texas and North Louisiana. An upper level ridge of high pressure also expanded northeast from Mexico across much of Texas. As a result, afternoon temperatures climbed into the mid to upper 90s over these areas. When combined with the high humidity, heat indices ranged from 110-119 degrees from the June 17th through the 20th. Although slightly cooler and less humid conditions moved into North Louisiana on June 21st, a 62 year old female was found dead in her home in Keithville (Southern Caddo Parish) following several days of no electricity after severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread power outages on June 16th. The Caddo Coroner's Office stated that the victim had suffered from previous health issues, and that the intense heat and humidity led to hyperthermia, and ultimately, her death.

Supercell thunderstorms developed toward midday across the middle Red River Valley along and south of a stalled frontal boundary. These thunderstorms progressed east southeast with upper 80s temperatures on the warm side of the boundary with dew points exceeding 75 degrees. Mid-level lapse rates of 7-8 C/km overspread this very moist low-level air mass over a rather broad west-to-east corridor, supporting extreme instability upwards of 4500 J/kg well downstream of the upscale-growing convection. Coinciding this corridor of extreme buoyancy was very strong effective bulk shear near 65 kts along with hodographs that were modestly curved at the low levels and elongated above 3 km AGL. Numerous local storm reports were documented of large hail and damaging wind gusts across parts of North Louisiana throughout the event. Unfortunately, a 73 year old male was killed in a fire ignited after lightning struck his home near Shongaloo in Northern Webster Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hen egg size hail reported just northeast of Quitman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late during the evening of June 15th, regional radar analysis showed several clusters of severe thunderstorms ongoing along the middle Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. The eastern-most cluster displayed signs of cold pool intensification and developed a bowing structure. Observational trends farther downstream favored a continued motion along the instability gradient to the southeast through midnight into the early morning hours on June 16th. Given the background wind fields and buoyancy were still quite strong, the threat of damaging winds upwards of 75+ mph and large hail increased as the complex moved into far Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana just after midnight.||Farther west near the Red River, additional storm development occurred along the trailing outflow. Less organized than the eastern cluster, individual storms, including a few supercells, eventually evolved and remained semi-discrete for a few hours. Given the more cellular nature, large hail was a threat initially with the amount of instability upwards of 5000 J/kg and 60 knots of effective shear. A greater risk of damaging winds gradually evolved as storms remained organized and grew upscale overnight.||As a result, large hail up to 2+ inches in diameter eventually occurred in some storms followed by an intense bow echo, resulting in widespread severe winds and even some hurricane-force gusts, setting the stage for initiating a derecho-type event across much of Northeast Texas and North Louisiana. In addition, an isolated tornado also occurred near the Texas/Louisiana state line on the south flank of the extreme derecho winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on Highway 151 near Fawn Drive close to Union and Ouachita Parish line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late during the evening of June 15th, regional radar analysis showed several clusters of severe thunderstorms ongoing along the middle Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. The eastern-most cluster displayed signs of cold pool intensification and developed a bowing structure. Observational trends farther downstream favored a continued motion along the instability gradient to the southeast through midnight into the early morning hours on June 16th. Given the background wind fields and buoyancy were still quite strong, the threat of damaging winds upwards of 75+ mph and large hail increased as the complex moved into far Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana just after midnight.||Farther west near the Red River, additional storm development occurred along the trailing outflow. Less organized than the eastern cluster, individual storms, including a few supercells, eventually evolved and remained semi-discrete for a few hours. Given the more cellular nature, large hail was a threat initially with the amount of instability upwards of 5000 J/kg and 60 knots of effective shear. A greater risk of damaging winds gradually evolved as storms remained organized and grew upscale overnight.||As a result, large hail up to 2+ inches in diameter eventually occurred in some storms followed by an intense bow echo, resulting in widespread severe winds and even some hurricane-force gusts, setting the stage for initiating a derecho-type event across much of Northeast Texas and North Louisiana. In addition, an isolated tornado also occurred near the Texas/Louisiana state line on the south flank of the extreme derecho winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed in the Point community area, with many trees across several roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed toward midday across the middle Red River Valley along and south of a stalled frontal boundary. These thunderstorms progressed east southeast with upper 80s temperatures on the warm side of the boundary with dew points exceeding 75 degrees. Mid-level lapse rates of 7-8 C/km overspread this very moist low-level air mass over a rather broad west-to-east corridor, supporting extreme instability upwards of 4500 J/kg well downstream of the upscale-growing convection. Coinciding this corridor of extreme buoyancy was very strong effective bulk shear near 65 kts along with hodographs that were modestly curved at the low levels and elongated above 3 km AGL. Numerous local storm reports were documented of large hail and damaging wind gusts across parts of North Louisiana throughout the event. Unfortunately, a 73 year old male was killed in a fire ignited after lightning struck his home near Shongaloo in Northern Webster Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported in Grayson via social media.

A greater risk of damaging winds gradually evolved as storms remained organized and grew upscale overnight.||As a result, large hail up to 2+ inches in diameter eventually occurred in some storms followed by an intense bow echo, resulting in widespread severe winds and even some hurricane-force gusts, setting the stage for initiating a derecho-type event across much of Northeast Texas and North Louisiana. In addition, an isolated tornado also occurred near the Texas/Louisiana state line on the south flank of the extreme derecho winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge of high pressure remained anchored over Central Mexico into west central Texas and Western Oklahoma on June 25th-26th. However, a weak surface front remained stationary from portions of Southeast Oklahoma into Western and Southwest Arkansas, as a shortwave trough rounding the top of the ridge axis shifted southeast along the stalled front. Ample surface and elevated instability and adequate moisture remained near this front, with large scale forcing associated with the trough enhancing scattered severe thunderstorm development across portions of McCurtain County Oklahoma into portions of extreme Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and much of Northwest Louisiana during the early morning hours of June 26th. Instances of large hail were reported initially, before transitioning to damaging winds as the storms accelerated into extreme Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and Northwest Louisiana. These storms then weakened as they exited the region by mid-morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed and utility poles snapped near 7th Street in Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on June 9th over Western and Central Kansas, near a shortwave trough that drifted east-southeast into the Southern Plains that evening. While this complex of storms weakened as it moved through Oklahoma during the overnight hours, showers and thunderstorms associated with the remnants of these storms re-intensified by mid and late morning on the 10th across extreme Southeast Oklahoma and Northeast Texas as moderate instability developed with the onset of daytime heating ahead of the trough. Despite the weak shear in place, these storms were able to organize into another MCS as it moved through East Texas and North Louisiana during the afternoon hours, producing multiple reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines, as well as isolated instances of large hail before exiting the region into Southeast Texas and Central Louisiana by early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail south of Highway 6.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed near Rodessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed toward midday across the middle Red River Valley along and south of a stalled frontal boundary. These thunderstorms progressed east southeast with upper 80s temperatures on the warm side of the boundary with dew points exceeding 75 degrees. Mid-level lapse rates of 7-8 C/km overspread this very moist low-level air mass over a rather broad west-to-east corridor, supporting extreme instability upwards of 4500 J/kg well downstream of the upscale-growing convection. Coinciding this corridor of extreme buoyancy was very strong effective bulk shear near 65 kts along with hodographs that were modestly curved at the low levels and elongated above 3 km AGL. Numerous local storm reports were documented of large hail and damaging wind gusts across parts of North Louisiana throughout the event. Unfortunately, a 73 year old male was killed in a fire ignited after lightning struck his home near Shongaloo in Northern Webster Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on top of a mobile home on Wise Road north of Plain Dealing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust convection developed across northern and central portions of Louisiana with multiple multicellular structures and supercells ahead of a surface cold front. Surface temperatures in the 90s along with dew points in the 70s contributed to 4000 J/kg MLCAPE. Strong mid-level flow also supported over 40 knots of effective bulk shear. As such, storms organized into multicellular clusters and transient supercells, with damaging gusts and large hail the main threats. Furthermore, MRMS MESH depicted storms with hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, with a couple of 1 inch hail/wind damage reports also received. Given the heated, well-mixed boundary layer (and associated 3500+ J/kg MLCAPE) preceding the storms, supercell structures persisted into the evening hours with a large hail/damaging gust threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported in Zwolle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms moved through East Texas and Northwest Louisiana during the early morning hours on June 16th. As it moved through East Texas and into the Shreveport metro area, the storm begin to take on a bow shape, indicating that the system would likely produce strong straight line winds. As the storm reached near the Texas/Louisiana state line from Panola County into southwest Caddo Parish, it produced a brief EF-1 tornado with maximum wind gusts of 110 mph, a path width of nearly 850 yards, and path length of nearly six miles in far southwest Caddo Parish. ||With a storm already producing widespread straight-line wind damage, it became difficult to determine what was tornadic and what was straight-line winds. It was determined that the tornado touched down to the west of County Road 322 in Panola County where a large tree fell onto a single family home. The tornado then moved to the southeast across the Texas/Louisiana state line towards Johns Gin Road where it produced widespread tree damage all along Johns Gin Road north of Keatchie Marshall Road. From there, the tornado moved through a neighborhood to the west of Four |Forks Road just north of Keatchie Marshall Road where a number of trees were either uprooted or snapped. This same area was just struck by a tragic tornado in December 2022. The tornado then tracked to the southeast along Keatchie Marshall Road where damage became more sparse and then lifted just to the west of Preston Road.

As such, storms organized into multicellular clusters and transient supercells, with damaging gusts and large hail the main threats. Furthermore, MRMS MESH depicted storms with hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, with a couple of 1 inch hail/wind damage reports also received. Given the heated, well-mixed boundary layer (and associated 3500+ J/kg MLCAPE) preceding the storms, supercell structures persisted into the evening hours with a large hail/damaging gust threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines downed in Pleasant Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines downed on Highway 6 between Fort Jessup and Robeline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed toward midday across the middle Red River Valley along and south of a stalled frontal boundary. These thunderstorms progressed east southeast with upper 80s temperatures on the warm side of the boundary with dew points exceeding 75 degrees. Mid-level lapse rates of 7-8 C/km overspread this very moist low-level air mass over a rather broad west-to-east corridor, supporting extreme instability upwards of 4500 J/kg well downstream of the upscale-growing convection. Coinciding this corridor of extreme buoyancy was very strong effective bulk shear near 65 kts along with hodographs that were modestly curved at the low levels and elongated above 3 km AGL. Numerous local storm reports were documented of large hail and damaging wind gusts across parts of North Louisiana throughout the event. Unfortunately, a 73 year old male was killed in a fire ignited after lightning struck his home near Shongaloo in Northern Webster Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and power poles reported broken via MPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late during the evening of June 15th, regional radar analysis showed several clusters of severe thunderstorms ongoing along the middle Red River Valley into Northeast Texas. The eastern-most cluster displayed signs of cold pool intensification and developed a bowing structure. Observational trends farther downstream favored a continued motion along the instability gradient to the southeast through midnight into the early morning hours on June 16th. Given the background wind fields and buoyancy were still quite strong, the threat of damaging winds upwards of 75+ mph and large hail increased as the complex moved into far Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana just after midnight.||Farther west near the Red River, additional storm development occurred along the trailing outflow. Less organized than the eastern cluster, individual storms, including a few supercells, eventually evolved and remained semi-discrete for a few hours. Given the more cellular nature, large hail was a threat initially with the amount of instability upwards of 5000 J/kg and 60 knots of effective shear. A greater risk of damaging winds gradually evolved as storms remained organized and grew upscale overnight.||As a result, large hail up to 2+ inches in diameter eventually occurred in some storms followed by an intense bow echo, resulting in widespread severe winds and even some hurricane-force gusts, setting the stage for initiating a derecho-type event across much of Northeast Texas and North Louisiana. In addition, an isolated tornado also occurred near the Texas/Louisiana state line on the south flank of the extreme derecho winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gas station canopy downed near the intersection of McArthur Drive and Polk Street in Mansfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours on June 9th over Western and Central Kansas, near a shortwave trough that drifted east-southeast into the Southern Plains that evening. While this complex of storms weakened as it moved through Oklahoma during the overnight hours, showers and thunderstorms associated with the remnants of these storms re-intensified by mid and late morning on the 10th across extreme Southeast Oklahoma and Northeast Texas as moderate instability developed with the onset of daytime heating ahead of the trough. Despite the weak shear in place, these storms were able to organize into another MCS as it moved through East Texas and North Louisiana during the afternoon hours, producing multiple reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines, as well as isolated instances of large hail before exiting the region into Southeast Texas and Central Louisiana by early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed throughout the Many area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) developed during the early morning hours of July 16th across North Texas, along a stationary front that extended from North Texas east to along the Southern Arkansas/North Louisiana border. Large scale forcing just ahead a weak upper level disturbance traversing atop upper ridging in place across Westcentral Texas maintained this complex of thunderstorms through the morning, with strong diurnal heating contributing to ample instability to intensify these thunderstorms as they entered East Texas, Southern Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the afternoon. Thus, this line of thunderstorms became severe, producing damaging winds which resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across North Louisiana before they exited the area by late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto power lines in the Princeton community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front drifted into the region while a trough dug south to the gulf. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed with some becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed in Eunice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper trough continued to extend from the Great Lakes Region and Mid-Mississippi Valley southwest into North Texas during the afternoon of July 3rd, while an associated weak surface front lifted back north before becoming stationary across Southeast Oklahoma and Western/Central Arkansas. Strong heating within a moist air mass resulted in strong instability developing during the afternoon, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough adequate enough for the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas, and Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon. One isolated severe thunderstorm developed over Downtown Shreveport, and produced a 58 mph wind gust at the Shreveport Downtown Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Shreveport Downtown Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low and frontal boundary stalled over northeast Texas on the afternoon of February 7th. An upper level low coming out of the desert southwest tracked eastward and drove a cold front towards the local area. At the same time, the surface low began to deepen and occlude while moving northeastward. Thunderstorms developed both ahead of the front in the warm sector and directly along the cold front as it passed through the local area. One storm produced an EF-2 tornado, another severe thunderstorm winds, and one produced flash flooding. No injuries with any storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported impassable streets across Bridge City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm over Lake Charles produced isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Common and College streets near the 210 overpass became impassable for a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced flooding and a landspout near Lebleu Settlement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of water covered Gillson Road about 1/3 of a mile from the Highway 383 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached the local area from the west, the environment became primed for thunderstorms to develop with warmth and instability increasing. Storms that developed ahead of the front had little mid level flow to give them much movement. Thus, one storm over the Baton Rouge metro produced heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos shared on social media by broadcast media showed water approximately 2 feet deep in the Sweetbriar trailer park on Samuels Road in Zachary. At least one car was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced flooding and a landspout near Lebleu Settlement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout touched down near the intersection of River and Clyde Herbert Roads and produced minor damage to a home and two outbuildings. The max estimated wind was 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of short waves moved across there region and interacted with the very hot and moist air mass that had been in place. Thunderstorms moved front the northwest to the southeast across the region and several became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast meteorologist passed along a report of 1 inch in diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low and frontal boundary stalled over northeast Texas on the afternoon of February 7th. An upper level low coming out of the desert southwest tracked eastward and drove a cold front towards the local area. At the same time, the surface low began to deepen and occlude while moving northeastward. Thunderstorms developed both ahead of the front in the warm sector and directly along the cold front as it passed through the local area. One storm produced an EF-2 tornado, another severe thunderstorm winds, and one produced flash flooding. No injuries with any storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture from the public on social media showed an AutoZone sign blown down as well as another pole/sign damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and unstable air mass devolved into scattered afternoon thunderstorms. A few storms became severe after most locations topped 100 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were snapped in a severe storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level ridge of high pressure over the Rockies and Southern Plains weakened its grip slightly on the Four State Region late in the day on July 19th through the 21st, although afternoon temperatures still climbed into the upper 90s each day. A weak stationary front that was present in the preceding days over Central and Eastern Oklahoma into Western and Central Arkansas had dissipated by the 20th, which allowed for better vertical mixing and thus, not as oppressively humid conditions. However, heat indices still ranged from 105-110 degrees across much of North Louisiana before a weak cool front moved through the region late on the 21st.

A southerly low level flow returned with the retreat of a weak cool front across East Texas and Northwest Louisiana on July 12th, as an upper level ridge of high pressure built east across much of Texas and Oklahoma. Thus, afternoon temperatures returned back into the mid and upper 90s over these areas, before even hotter conditions were observed during the afternoon of the 13th. When combined with the high humidity, heat indices ranged from 105-110 degrees on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous storm system moved across the region during the 24th spawning flooding, high wind gusts, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gum Slough Tornado touched down just south of Line Road, and merged with the Nibletts Bluff Tornado. Trees were damaged while sections of roofs were taken off of a mobile home and an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds along the coast behind a cold front pushed tides below -1 foot MLLW for multiple hours along the coast of Cameron Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds pushed tides to -2.15 feet below mean lower low water at Sabine Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moved across the area while an upper level disturbance passed to the north. Thunderstorms occurred across the region during both the 8th and 9th with a couple storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started along Highway 103 southwest of Grand Prairie and moved to the northeast to Highway 748 where it ripped a roof from a well constructed home and snapped several large hardwood trees where the highest wind speeds were estimated. The tornado continued northeast causing damage to trees and outbuildings and minor home damage. Maximum estimated wind speeds were 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure remained anchored over the Southern Plains on July 13th, although a portion of its influence extended farther east into the Lower Mississippi Valley. A stationary front extended from Central and Eastern Oklahoma into Western and Southern Arkansas, with low level moisture pooling near and south of the front contributing to heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees each afternoon through the 18th as high temperatures climbed into the mid 90s to near 100 degrees.

A remnant outflow boundary made its way over southern Mississippi during the morning. Once daytime heating increased, convection formed along that boundary. The convection formed a line and surged out across southeast Louisiana, coastal Mississippi, and the marine waters before dying out at sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along LA-965 and within the low water bridge area due to thunderstorm winds. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive and slow-moving ridge sat over the southern portion of the CONUS at the end of July. This ridge caused extreme record-breaking temperatures across much of the south, including southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. The record-breaking temperatures paired with high humidity values caused the heat to be excessive for multiple days at the end of July across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred much of southeast Louisiana, mainly along I-12 and northward  during on July 30th. High temperatures were in the upper 90s to 100 degrees with heat indices were in the 113 to 115 degree range each afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive and slow-moving ridge sat over the southern portion of the CONUS at the end of July. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough extended southwest from the Great Lakes Region southwest into Southwest Arkansas and Northeast Texas during the afternoon of July 6th, which reinforced a weak surface front south into the I-30 corridor of Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas. Meanwhile, a strong seabreeze pushed inland across East Texas and North Louisiana during the late afternoon through the evening hours, allowing showers and thunderstorms to spread north into these areas. Strong daytime heating and large scale ascent along the slow moving front contributed to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms along and south of the front across portions of East Texas and North Louisiana, and collided with the northward advancing storms associated with the seabreeze during the late afternoon and early evening hours, with a cluster of showers and thunderstorms redeveloping over the same areas from Harrison County Texas into Southern Caddo and Bossier Parishes in Northwest Louisiana. Given the very moist air mass in place, these slow moving storms were efficient rainfall producers, contributing to areas of flash flooding that were observed over these locations. Localized pockets of 2-5 inch rains fell over far South Shreveport and Southern Caddo Parish in a short period of time, with multiple roads flooded before the storms diminished by mid and late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several cars stalled in high water in the Provenance neighborhood in extreme Southeast Shreveport. One business was also flooded in this area.

The record-breaking temperatures paired with high humidity values caused the heat to be excessive across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred over portions of southeast Louisiana mainly along and north of I-12 on July 13th. High temperatures were in the upper 90s to 100 degrees with heat indices were in the 113 to 115 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge sat over the southwestern portion of the CONUS in the middle of July. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred over portions of southeast Louisiana mainly along and north of I-12 on July 13th. High temperatures were in the upper 90s to 100 degrees with heat indices were in the 113 to 115 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure remained anchored over the Southern Plains on July 13th, although a portion of its influence extended farther east into the Lower Mississippi Valley. A stationary front extended from Central and Eastern Oklahoma into Western and Southern Arkansas, with low level moisture pooling near and south of the front inhibiting mixing of the air mass as temperatures climbed into the mid to upper 90s across Western Louisiana. This resulted in heat indices ranging from 110-115 degrees across Western Louisiana, before showers and thunderstorms reinforced the surface front back south into extreme Northeast Texas and Southern Arkansas on the 14th, contributing to slightly less humid conditions. Unfortunately, a 34 year old male was found dead from an apparent heat stroke at 3:35 pm on the 13th. His body was found in an unairconditioned camper in the 400 block of Rutherford Street in the Highland neighborhood in Shreveport. The maximum heat index recorded that afternoon at the Shreveport Regional Airport on the 13th was 115 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure remained anchored over the Southern Plains on July 13th, although a portion of its influence extended farther east into the Lower Mississippi Valley. A stationary front extended from Central and Eastern Oklahoma into Western and Southern Arkansas, with low level moisture pooling near and south of the front contributing to heat indices ranging from 105-110 degrees each afternoon through the 18th as high temperatures climbed into the mid 90s to near 100 degrees.
Drier air aloft was in place during the afternoon, and only a few cells were able to form. The dominant one that formed used that dry air to produce damaging gusty winds over southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple oak trees had limb damage near Hwy 405 and Cabereelo Road due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at Lafayette during the month of August was 90.3F which broke the old record of 87.1F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 110F on the 27th which broke the previous all time high record of 107F set on July 13, 1901. Lafayette also experienced the 7th driest August ever recorded. Lafayette Parish and greater Acadiana was under an excessive heat warning for 18 days of the month and only went without any heat product for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were observed developing along a nearly stalled frontal boundary and remnant outflows across parts of Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas, and Northern Louisiana on the afternoon of August 27th. Very strong diurnal heating was sufficient to erode a capping inversion aloft with around 1500-2000 J/kg of mean-layer instability supporting occasional stronger updrafts. Deep-layer vertical shear across much of the region was weak at generally less than 10-15 knots. This was still enough to support multi-cellular, pulse convection as the primary storm mode. Weak surface flow was also insufficient to support much cold pool propagation. Therefore, the primary severe risk was tied to individual pulse downdrafts as thunderstorms collapsed. This resulted in numerous damaging wind reports across parts of the region, including several reports in parts of Caddo Parish and one in Natchitoches Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin roofing was peeled back and removed from a structure near Highway 486 and U.S. Highway 71 between Clarence and Campti.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge of high pressure intensified and expanded farther northeast from the Southern Plains into the mid-section of U.S. through much of mid to late August. Prevailing southerly flow south of the ridge maintained persistent Gulf of Mexico moisture across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid to late August.

An upper ridge of high pressure strengthened farther into Oklahoma and Texas through mid August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front remained stationary across portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid August.

An upper ridge of high pressure intensified over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through early August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front lifted back north into portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over all of the Four State Region. Very strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the lower triple digits across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, heat indices ranged from 110 to 115 degrees each day throughout much of the first week of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions and very high temperatures led to very dry fuels across the region. Several wildfires ignited in the area by the end of the month. The largest wildfires occurred in the piney woods of Beauregard, Vernon, and Rapides Parishes, however many other smaller fires occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four main wildfires occurred in Vernon Parish at the end of August with over 9,000 acres burned. One home and multiple outbuildings were burned in the Ida Fire with approximately 3,628 acres affected. The Highway 113 Fire was along the Vernon and Rapides Parish line with more than half inside of Vernon Parish. It burned just over 7,124 acres burned in total of pine forest. The Lion's Camp Road Fire did not destroy any homes, however 785 acres burned of pine forest. The Miller Fire was along the Sabine River and burned over 1.300 acres of forest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two main wildfires occurred during the end of August in Rapides Parish. The Elizabeth fire burned one home and roughly 940 acres of pine forest while the Claiborne Fire burned 1,600 acres of pine forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions and very high temperatures led to very dry fuels across the region. Several wildfires ignited in the area by the end of the month. The largest wildfires occurred in the piney woods of Beauregard, Vernon, and Rapides Parishes, however many other smaller fires occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tiger Island Wildfire began between Merryville, DeRidder, and Singer and affected mostly pine forest. The area affected had ample dry fuels from the exceptional drought and downed trees from Hurricane Laura. The fire spread to over 31,000 acres and caused evacuations of Merryville and a portion of Singer and DeRidder. Approximately 7 homes and 39 structures altogether burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at New Iberia during the month of August was 89.5F which broke the old record of 86.6F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the previous all time high record of 106F just set on the 26th. New Iberia also experienced the driest August ever recorded. Acadiana was under an excessive heat warning for 18 days of the month and only went without any heat product for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were observed developing along a nearly stalled frontal boundary and remnant outflows across parts of Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas, and Northern Louisiana on the afternoon of August 27th. Very strong diurnal heating was sufficient to erode a capping inversion aloft with around 1500-2000 J/kg of mean-layer instability supporting occasional stronger updrafts. Deep-layer vertical shear across much of the region was weak at generally less than 10-15 knots. This was still enough to support multi-cellular, pulse convection as the primary storm mode. Weak surface flow was also insufficient to support much cold pool propagation. Therefore, the primary severe risk was tied to individual pulse downdrafts as thunderstorms collapsed. This resulted in numerous damaging wind reports across parts of the region, including several reports in parts of Caddo Parish and one in Natchitoches Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed, power poles snapped and power lines downed along the 10000 block of East Kings Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby New Iberia during the month of August was 89.5F which broke the old record of 86.6F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the previous all time high record of 106F just set on the 26th. Acadiana was under an excessive heat warning for 18 days of the month and only went without any heat product for 4 days of the month.

This lead to the rapid onset of drought conditions during the beginning of August and lasting through the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the zone was under D2 to D4 drought due to minimal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another typical summertime convective regime was in place for the 26th. With the persistent ridging over the area, the convection had more dry air to work with, so the stronger storms were able to produce damaging winds across parts of southeast Louisiana  before dissipating shortly after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage, as well as damage to a metal building from downburst thunderstorm winds reported by media on Denham Road in Gonzalez. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby New Iberia during the month of August was 89.5F which broke the old record of 86.6F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the previous all time high record of 106F just set on the 26th. Acadiana was under an excessive heat warning for 18 days of the month and only went without any heat product for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With ridging sitting over the area for the better part of August, all of southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi saw significantly less rainfall than usual for August. This lead to the rapid onset of drought conditions during the beginning of August and lasting through the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the zone was under D2 to D4 drought due to minimal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive ridge sat over the southern portion of the CONUS. This ridge caused extreme record-breaking temperatures across much of the south, including southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. The record-breaking temperatures paired with high humidity values caused the heat to be excessive across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred for all of southeast Louisiana on August 14th. High temperatures were in the upper 90s to 100 degrees with heat indices were in the 113 to 115 degree range each afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge of high pressure persisted over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through the first week of August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front shifted south into the Middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over much of the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath the upper ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing into the mid and upper 90s across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, max heat indices ranged from 105 to 109 degrees on August 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a dominant upper-level ridge in place, a few isolated severe thunderstorms were able to develop across parts of Northwest Louisiana on the afternoon of August 25th. Strong diurnal heating allowed for mean-layer instability around 1500-2000 J/kg while deep-layer shear was rather weak and generally less than 10-15 knots. Despite the shear limitations, pulse convection resulted in a few isolated severe thunderstorms across parts of Sabine and Natchitoches Parish around mid-afternoon. Downdraft instability values around 1000 J/kg and fairly steep low-level lapse rates, resulting from very hot temperatures at the surface, also likely supported stronger outflow winds with some sporadic damaging wind gusts. Therefore, isolated reports of damaging wind gusts in Sabine and Natchitoches Parish were likely a result of individual pulse downdrafts as these thunderstorms collapsed within a layer of drier air beneath their cloud bases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines downed from near Many, south and southwest across Sabine Parish to the west of Florien to east of the Clare community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of Northern Louisiana at the very end of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge of high pressure persisted over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through the first week of August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front shifted south into the Middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over much of the Four State Region. Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of Northern Louisiana at the very end of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front drifted into Central Louisiana during a heat wave. The very hot and unstable airmass ahead of the boundary produced a round of severe weather with high wind gusts and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five homes, a few out buildings were lightly damaged from downburst winds. Numerous trees were also damaged or downed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of North Central Louisiana during the last week of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of North Central Louisiana during the last week of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

An upper ridge of high pressure strengthened farther into Oklahoma and Texas through mid August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front remained stationary across portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid August.

An upper ridge of high pressure intensified over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through early August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front lifted back north into portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over all of the Four State Region. Very strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the lower triple digits across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, heat indices ranged from 110 to 115 degrees each day throughout much of the first week of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2 which broke the old record of 87.0 set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th  which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. Lake Charles also experienced the 5th driest August ever recorded. Calcasieu Parish and greater Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of North Central Louisiana during the last week of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

An upper ridge of high pressure strengthened farther into Oklahoma and Texas through mid August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front remained stationary across portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid August.

An upper ridge of high pressure intensified over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through early August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front lifted back north into portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over all of the Four State Region. Very strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the lower triple digits across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, heat indices ranged from 110 to 115 degrees each day throughout much of the first week of August.

The record-breaking temperatures paired with high humidity values caused the heat to be excessive across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred for all of southeast Louisiana on August 9th. High temperatures were in the upper 90s to 100 degrees with heat indices were in the 113 to 115 degree range each afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. Oakdale also hit 109 degrees during the afternoon of the 27th. Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to Exceptional (D4) drought conditions developed across Sabine Parish in West Central Louisiana throughout the month of August. This was in response to a flash drought that resulted from an exceptionally hot and dry summer, beginning in mid-June with well below normal rainfall and an extended period of triple-digit high temperatures through much of July and August. Total rainfall amounts during much of the summer period were only near 25% of normal or less. This dryness contributed to lower than normal streamflow observed across area creeks, streams, and rivers through the end of August.

An upper ridge of high pressure intensified over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through early August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front lifted back north into portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over all of the Four State Region. Very strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the lower triple digits across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, heat indices ranged from 110 to 115 degrees each day throughout much of the first week of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Alexandria during the month of August was 91.4F which broke the old record of 87.7F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 110F on the 19th and 24th which was the hottest temperature ever recorded.  Central Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 22 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month, the only 4 days when the actual temperature failed to reach 100F or more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at Alexandria during the month of August was 91.4F which broke the old record of 87.7F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 110F on the 19th and 24th which was the hottest temperature ever recorded. Daily records were set on 20 days. Alexandria also experienced the driest month ever recorded. Central Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 22 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month, the only 4 days when the actual temperature failed to reach 100F or more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge of high pressure intensified over the Southern Plains and extended east into the Ozarks and Central Arkansas through early August. Meanwhile, a weak surface front lifted back north into portions of Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas, with moisture pooling observed near this front over all of the Four State Region. Very strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the lower triple digits across North Louisiana and limited mixing of dewpoints observed. Thus, heat indices ranged from 110 to 115 degrees each day throughout much of the first week of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. DeRidder also hit 109F on the 27th. Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge of high pressure intensified and expanded farther northeast from the Southern Plains into the mid-section of U.S. through much of mid to late August. Prevailing southerly flow south of the ridge maintained persistent Gulf of Mexico moisture across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid to late August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. Jennings hit 110F on the 19th, and Jennings and Lake Arthur hit 107F and 106F respectively on the 27th. Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge of high pressure intensified and expanded farther northeast from the Southern Plains into the mid-section of U.S. through much of mid to late August. Prevailing southerly flow south of the ridge maintained persistent Gulf of Mexico moisture across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid to late August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime convective regime was in place for the 27th. However, the air was slightly cooler aloft and that helped promote hail in the strongest storms across southeast Louisiana along with a damaging wind threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station KHDC Hammond recorded a wind gust of 59 mph or 51 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple wildfires ignited across Southern Sabine Parish during the day on August 25, 2023, with a large wildfire affecting the Pirates Cove community in extreme Southwest Sabine Parish along Toledo Bend Reservoir just west of the Toro community. Over 200 acres burned from this fire, with 9 homes/structures lost in the fire but fortunately, no injuries or fatalities were reported. Multiple homes were evacuated in this community, before the fire was fully contained later that evening from multiple fire departments with assistance from dozers and helicopters from the Louisiana Dept. of Forestry. ||This wildfire occurred within a broad area where exceptional (D4) drought conditions were observed during late-August, while daily high temperatures soared to well over 100 degrees and minimum relative humidity values dropped to near 20% or less. Although wind speeds were not particularly high, the combination of critically to extremely dry vegetation and very low relative humidity values provided nearly ideal conditions for rapid ignition and spreading of wildfires.

Wildfires occurred on the afternoon of August 14, 2023, near the Florien and Mount Carmel communities in southeast Sabine Parish. Approximately 2,100 acres along with eighteen structures were damaged or destroyed, including 10 residential homes. No injuries or fatalities were reported, and the wildfires were fully contained by later that evening. Evacuations were ordered for some residents along State Highway 118 and several other parish roads which were closed to traffic, and the Florien Civic Center was opened to evacuees. A command center was also set up at the Florien Fire Station where water and food was available for anyone displaced by the fires. It is suspected that the cause of the fires was a spark from a downed utility pole on Mt. Carmel Cutoff Road.||This wildfire occurred within a broad area where severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions were observed during mid-August, while daily high temperatures soared to well over 100 degrees and minimum relative humidity values dropped to near 20% or less. Although wind speeds were not particularly high, the combination of critically to extremely dry vegetation and very low relative humidity values provided nearly ideal conditions for rapid ignition and spreading of wildfires.

A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Most climate sites tied or broke streaks of 100 degree days and all sites set all time highs. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. Numerous deaths are suspected across Louisiana due to heat stress. The very hot and dry conditions aggravated the already dry conditions pushing much of the region into an exceptional drought. Multiple wildfires began during the middle of the month as a result. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Lake Charles during the month of August was 89.2F which broke the old record of 87.0F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 109F on the 27th which broke the all time high record of 107F set on August 31, 2000. Jennings hit 110F on the 19th, and Jennings and Lake Arthur hit 107F and 106F respectively on the 27th. Southwest Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 14 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic heat wave was observed across much of the deep south during the month of August. Apparent temperatures each afternoon for the month reached over 105 degrees with most afternoons rising into the 110 to 120 degree Fahrenheit range. The largest and longest lasting of the wildfires was the Tiger Island Wildfire in Beauregard Parish which burned over 31,000 acres of pine forest across an area with many downed trees from Hurricane Laura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The average temperature at nearby Alexandria during the month of August was 91.4F which broke the old record of 87.7F set in 2011. The hottest temperature during the month was 110F on the 19th and 24th which was the hottest temperature ever recorded. Leesville hit 109F on the 24th. Central Louisiana was under an excessive heat warning for 22 days of the month and only went without any heat products for 4 days of the month, the only 4 days when the actual temperature failed to reach 100F or more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge of high pressure intensified and expanded farther northeast from the Southern Plains into the mid-section of U.S. through much of mid to late August. Prevailing southerly flow south of the ridge maintained persistent Gulf of Mexico moisture across the Four State Region. Strong sinking motion observed beneath this ridge resulted in compressional warming, with temperatures climbing well into the triple digits across all of North Louisiana. Given the limited mixing of dewpoints observed, oppressive heat was observed with heat indices reaching or exceeding 110 degrees throughout much of mid to late August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fueled with the exceptional drought conditions, a small brush fire near Folsom quickly spread towards a house. A woman was caught outside as the fire spread, and was fatally injured by the fire before the fire spread to the nearby home. Shortly after, fire fighters were able to contain the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small brush fire started in Folsom quickly spread to a nearby house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Likely aided by the ongoing drought conditions, a small fire spread to nearby buildings in Washington Parish, where a man was fatally injured before fire fighters put out the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72-year-old veteran was fatally burned when an outside fire spread to their building.

This lead to the rapid onset of drought conditions during the beginning of August and lasting through the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the zone was under D2 to D4 drought due to minimal rainfall.
